title: G Is for Glasses: An A-to-Z Glossary of Eyewear Terms
description: From astigmatism to Zeiss lenses — a fun, plain-English glossary of every glasses term you'll encounter at the optician. Bookmark this one.
tldr: A comprehensive but readable glossary covering 50+ eyewear terms from A to Z. Written in plain English so you can walk into any optical store and understand what they're talking about.

definition.text: A comprehensive reference of eyewear terminology — from lens types and frame materials to prescription abbreviations and coating technologies — explained in everyday language.

sections.content:
Acetate: A plant-based plastic used to make most fashion eyeglass frames. It can be made in virtually any colour or pattern (including tortoiseshell) and is lighter than older plastics. Most frames from brands like Ray-Ban and Gucci use acetate.

Anti-Reflective (AR) Coating: A lens coating that reduces reflections on the lens surface. Without it, about 8% of light bounces off your lenses instead of passing through to your eyes. AR coating improves clarity, reduces glare from headlights at night, and makes your glasses look better in photos.

Astigmatism: A common refractive error caused by an irregularly shaped cornea (football-shaped instead of round). It causes blurred or distorted vision at all distances. Corrected with a "cylinder" value in your prescription.

Axis: The angle (1–180 degrees) that tells the lab where to orient the astigmatism correction in your lens. Always paired with cylinder.

Bifocal: A lens with two distinct vision zones — distance on top, reading on the bottom — separated by a visible line. Invented by Benjamin Franklin. Largely replaced by progressives in modern eyewear, but still preferred by some people for the clear, defined transition.

Blue Light: High-energy visible light (380–500nm wavelength) emitted by screens, LED bulbs, and sunlight. Blue light glasses filter a portion of this light. See our full guide on blue light glasses.

Bridge: The part of the frame that sits across your nose, connecting the two lenses. Bridge width (measured in mm) is one of three key frame measurements and determines how the glasses rest on your face.

This is one of the most common questions that comes up during eye exams, and the answer often depends on individual factors that only a professional assessment can evaluate. What works for one person may not be ideal for another — your prescription, daily activities, facial anatomy, and budget all play a role in determining the best approach.

If you're in the Edmonton area, Charm Optical offers comprehensive eye exams that go beyond just measuring your prescription. Their optometrists assess your overall eye health, discuss your visual needs, and recommend lens and frame options tailored to your lifestyle. With direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers and same-day glasses for many prescriptions, getting the right eyewear doesn't have to be complicated or expensive. Book online — appointments are usually available within a few days.

CR-39: The standard plastic lens material. Lighter than glass, decent optics, and about half the cost of high-index lenses. If nobody specifies a lens material, you're probably getting CR-39.

Cylinder (CYL): The prescription value that corrects astigmatism. Written as a number with a minus sign (e.g., -1.25). Higher numbers mean more astigmatism correction. Not everyone has a cylinder value — if your cornea is regularly shaped, this box is blank.

Cat-Eye: A frame shape with upswept outer corners that creates a lifted, vintage-inspired look. Works on round and square faces by adding angular definition to the upper face.

Coating: Any treatment applied to a lens surface — anti-reflective, scratch-resistant, hydrophobic (water-repelling), or blue light filtering. Most modern lenses come with at least a basic coating stack.

This is one of the most common questions that comes up during eye exams, and the answer often depends on individual factors that only a professional assessment can evaluate. What works for one person may not be ideal for another — your prescription, daily activities, facial anatomy, and budget all play a role in determining the best approach.

Dioptre (D): The unit of measurement for lens power. Your prescription is written in dioptres. A -2.00D lens corrects for 2 dioptres of myopia (nearsightedness). Higher numbers = stronger prescription = thicker lenses (unless you go high-index).

Distance Vision: Your ability to see things far away. Corrected by the main sphere value in your prescription. If you're nearsighted (myopic), you need distance correction. The top portion of bifocal and progressive lenses is dedicated to distance vision.

Digital Eye Strain: Tired eyes, headaches, dry eyes, and blurred vision from extended screen use. Caused more by reduced blinking and sustained close focus than by blue light specifically. The 20-20-20 rule helps.

This is one of the most common questions that comes up during eye exams, and the answer often depends on individual factors that only a professional assessment can evaluate. sections.h2: E–F – Eye Exam to Frame Materials
sections.content:
Eye Exam: A comprehensive assessment of your vision and eye health performed by an optometrist. In Alberta, the exam covers visual acuity, eye pressure, retinal health, and binocular vision. Alberta Health covers annual exams for children under 19 and adults 65+. Book an eye exam at Charm Optical.

Farsightedness (Hyperopia): Difficulty seeing things up close while distance vision remains clear. Corrected with plus-powered (+) lenses. Common in children (who often outgrow it) and adults over 40.

Frame: The structure that holds your lenses. Comes in full-rim, semi-rimless, and rimless styles. Materials include acetate, titanium, stainless steel, and TR-90 nylon.

Fitting Height: The vertical measurement from the bottom of the lens to your pupil centre. Critical for progressive lenses — if this number is off, the reading zone won't line up properly.

This is one of the most common questions that comes up during eye exams, and the answer often depends on individual factors that only a professional assessment can evaluate. What works for one person may not be ideal for another — your prescription, daily activities, facial anatomy, and budget all play a role in determining the best approach.

If you're in the Edmonton area, Charm Optical offers comprehensive eye exams that go beyond just measuring your prescription. Their optometrists assess your overall eye health, discuss your visual needs, and recommend lens and frame options tailored to your lifestyle. With direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers and same-day glasses for many prescriptions, getting the right eyewear doesn't have to be complicated or expensive. Book online — appointments are usually available within a few days.

Glasses (G is for Glasses, after all): Optical devices consisting of lenses mounted in a frame, worn on the face to correct refractive errors or protect the eyes. Also called spectacles, eyeglasses, or specs. An estimated 75% of Canadian adults use some form of vision correction.

Glare: Reflected light that reduces visual comfort and clarity. Managed with anti-reflective coatings on clear lenses or polarization on sunglasses.

High-Index Lenses: Lens materials that bend light more efficiently than standard CR-39 plastic. Available in 1.60, 1.67, and 1.74 index values. Higher index = thinner, lighter lens for the same prescription. Recommended for prescriptions above +/- 3.00. Pricier than standard lenses but worth it for comfort and aesthetics.

This is one of the most common questions that comes up during eye exams, and the answer often depends on individual factors that only a professional assessment can evaluate. What works for one person may not be ideal for another — your prescription, daily activities, facial anatomy, and budget all play a role in determining the best approach.

IPD (Interpupillary Distance): The distance between the centres of your two pupils, measured in millimetres. Average adult IPD is 54–74mm. This measurement ensures your lenses are centred correctly — misaligned optical centres cause eye strain and headaches.

Lens: The transparent optical element that bends light to correct your vision. Made from glass (rare now), CR-39 plastic, polycarbonate, or high-index materials. The lens is where most of the technology (and cost) in a pair of glasses lives.

Light-Adaptive (Photochromic): Lenses that darken automatically in sunlight and clear up indoors. Transitions is the most popular brand. Convenient for people who move between indoor and outdoor environments frequently.

This is one of the most common questions that comes up during eye exams, and the answer often depends on individual factors that only a professional assessment can evaluate. What works for one person may not be ideal for another — your prescription, daily activities, facial anatomy, and budget all play a role in determining the best approach.

If you're in the Edmonton area, Charm Optical offers comprehensive eye exams that go beyond just measuring your prescription. With direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers and same-day glasses for many prescriptions, getting the right eyewear doesn't have to be complicated or expensive. Book online — appointments are usually available within a few days.

Myopia (Nearsightedness): Close objects are clear, distant objects are blurry. The most common refractive error worldwide, affecting roughly 30% of Canadians. Corrected with minus-powered (-) lenses. Tends to develop in childhood and stabilize in early adulthood.

Nearsightedness: See Myopia above.

Nose Pads: Small silicone or plastic cushions on metal frames that rest on the sides of your nose. Adjustable nose pads allow for fit customization. Replace them when they turn yellow or lose grip.

OD (Oculus Dexter): Latin for "right eye." The right eye row on your prescription.

OS (Oculus Sinister): Latin for "left eye." The left eye row on your prescription.

OU (Oculus Uterque): Latin for "both eyes." Used when a prescription value applies to both eyes equally.

This is one of the most common questions that comes up during eye exams, and the answer often depends on individual factors that only a professional assessment can evaluate. What works for one person may not be ideal for another — your prescription, daily activities, facial anatomy, and budget all play a role in determining the best approach.

If you're in the Edmonton area, Charm Optical offers comprehensive eye exams that go beyond just measuring your prescription. Their optometrists assess your overall eye health, discuss your visual needs, and recommend lens and frame options tailored to your lifestyle. With direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers and same-day glasses for many prescriptions, getting the right eyewear doesn't have to be complicated or expensive. Book online — appointments are usually available within a few days.

PD: See IPD. Same measurement, different abbreviation.

Polycarbonate: A lens material that's thinner and significantly more impact-resistant than CR-39. Standard for children's glasses, safety glasses, and sports eyewear. Slightly lower optical clarity than CR-39 or high-index, but the safety advantage is worth it for active use.

Presbyopia: The gradual, age-related loss of close-up focusing ability. Starts around age 40, affects virtually everyone by 50. This is why people start needing reading glasses or progressives in their 40s. Not a disease — it's normal aging of the eye's lens.

Progressive Lenses: Multifocal lenses that provide a smooth transition from distance to intermediate to reading vision — no visible line. The modern replacement for bifocals. Require precise fitting measurements. See our progressive glasses guide.

Rimless: Frames with no material surrounding the lens. Lenses are held by screws or pins connecting directly to the bridge and temples. Lightest and most invisible style, but less durable. See our frame types guide.

Sphere (SPH): The main power of your lens, correcting for nearsightedness (minus values) or farsightedness (plus values). The most important number on your prescription.

Single Vision: A lens with one uniform power across the entire surface. Corrects for one distance — either far, intermediate, or near. The most common and simplest lens type. Same-day single vision lenses are available at many optical stores, including Charm Optical.

Scratch-Resistant Coating: A hard coating applied to lens surfaces that reduces (but doesn't eliminate) scratching. Standard on most modern lenses. No lens is truly scratch-proof.

Temple: The arms of the frame that extend from the hinges to behind your ears. Temple length (in mm) is the third number in frame sizing.

Titanium: A premium frame material that's lightweight, hypoallergenic, and corrosion-resistant. More expensive than stainless steel but noticeably lighter and more durable. Popular in Japanese-made frames.

UV Protection: Lenses that block ultraviolet radiation (UVA and UVB). Essential for sunglasses and increasingly included in clear prescription lenses. Long-term UV exposure contributes to cataracts and macular degeneration. All quality sunglasses from brands like Oakley and Maui Jim block 100% of UV.

Varifocal: Another name for progressive lenses, commonly used in the UK and Europe.

Wraparound: Frames that curve around the side of the face, providing peripheral coverage. Common in sports eyewear and safety glasses. The curved design makes standard flat lenses distort at the edges — wraparound-specific lenses correct for this.

Zeiss: Carl Zeiss is a German optics company that manufactures some of the highest-quality lens materials and coatings available. Many independent opticians offer Zeiss lenses as a premium option.

faqs.q: What does OD and OS mean on my prescription?
faqs.a: OD stands for Oculus Dexter (right eye) and OS stands for Oculus Sinister (left eye). They're Latin abbreviations used on every eyeglass prescription. Your right eye is always listed first.

faqs.q: What's the difference between polycarbonate and high-index lenses?
faqs.a: Polycarbonate is impact-resistant (great for kids and sports) while high-index is thinner and lighter (great for strong prescriptions). For safety, polycarbonate wins. For aesthetics and comfort with a strong prescription, high-index wins. Your optician at Charm Optical can recommend the best fit.

faqs.q: What does the sphere number on my prescription mean?
faqs.a: Sphere (SPH) is the main lens power. A minus number (like -2.50) corrects nearsightedness. A plus number (like +1.75) corrects farsightedness. Higher numbers mean a stronger prescription. Zero or blank means no correction needed for that measurement.

faqs.q: Where can I get glasses in Edmonton?
faqs.a: Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton carries 500+ designer frames. They offer comprehensive eye exams, same-day glasses, professional fitting, and direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers. Book online at see.charmoptical.ca.

faqs.q: Does insurance cover glasses in Alberta?
faqs.a: Most employer health plans cover $200-$500 toward glasses every 1-2 years. Alberta Blue Cross, Canada Life, and Desjardins are common providers. Charm Optical direct-bills most Alberta insurers so you only pay the difference.

ctaTitle: Have Questions? We Speak Plain English
ctaText: The team at Charm Optical is happy to explain any eyewear term, walk you through your prescription, or help you understand your options. No jargon, no pressure.
